next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 11

Marcia ironed the blue blouse with the steam turned off because the iron spat brown water when she trusted it. The apartment had already gone hot. Delroy slept flat on his back with his bad arm propped on a folded towel, mouth open, the sharp stink of liniment and sweat spreading off him as steadily as heat off pavement. Tiana's document folder stood upright on the milk crate beside her mattress, squared and orderly, as if papers could shame the world into behaving.

Marcia slid the photocopy of the red code notice, the last rent receipt, and the landlord's text into her handbag. She put in a pen that still worked and the envelope where she kept whatever cash could survive not being touched. The envelope felt weightless. That was the first bad sign of the day, though the day had started with Delroy trying and failing to get his T-shirt over his head without waking himself up from the pain.

He opened one eye when she snapped her bag shut.

"You going now?"

"Before office open proper. Maybe he'll be in a better mood if nobody else reach him first."

Delroy pushed himself up too fast and sucked air through his teeth. Even that sound annoyed her because it carried so much need in it. He looked tall even folded by pain, shoulder wrapped in the drugstore sling Tiana had improvised from an old scarf because the clinic wanted money before better equipment. His hair had flattened on one side and stood up wild on the other. Last week she might have loved him for how ridiculous that looked. This week it just looked expensive.

"I can come," he said. "Man to man sometim...
open_hooks_to_carry: The job is materially specific rather than generically hard.
Claude is implicated in the labor system without becoming its mastermind.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...because he wanted to hide forever. Because he could not bear to lay another maybe in her lap tonight and ask her to hold it carefully.

Micah was in the shower. Tiana was drying the cooler with a dish towel. Marcia sat with Celia's key beside her hand and a notepad pulled close, already writing columns: agency, rent, landlord, church sister who knew somebody at Jackson, numbers turned into a ladder because she did not know any other way to climb.

Delroy went to the closet and pulled out his old work boots. The leather was cracked white at the bend and one lace had been knotted back together three times. He set them by the door where he could grab them before dawn.

Marcia glanced at the boots, then at him.

"You found something?"

He kept his face plain. "Maybe."

Her eyes held on him for a beat, measuring whether maybe was worth the space it occupied. Then she went back to the page.

"Wake me if you leave early," she said.

"I'll."

That too might have been a lie. He had not decided yet.

He sat on the edge of the bed and listened to the shower run, to Tiana humming under her breath without knowing she was doing it, to Marcia turning pages in the notepad as if the next blank sheet might finally contain a number that behaved. Salt dried on his skin. Sand scratched the backs of his knees. His shoulder pulsed with its own weather.

For a few hours they had been a family at the beach. Not healed. Not fixed. Just held apart from the machinery that waited for them on land. Delroy let himself keep that much. Then he set the alarm for 4:18 and put the phone face down in the dark.
state_deltas_required: Replace the stolen document money and prove Delroy remains useful.
Delroy tears the shoulder badly and comes home nearly unpaid, narrowing the family's options further.
